explanation.rule: The suffix -ity (and its variant -ty) is added to adjectives to form nouns expressing a quality, state, or condition. These nouns are common in academic and formal contexts.

groups.note: Added to adjectives to form nouns. Note spelling changes: final -e is often dropped (mature → maturity); -able/-ible becomes -ability/-ibility (capable → capability, possible → possibility).

examples.definition: the ability or power to do something
the state of having the same rights and opportunities
the state of being accountable for something
the use of imagination to produce original ideas
the state of being fully developed or grown

wordForms.definition: the ability or power to do something
wordForms.example: The new software has the capability to process large files instantly.
wordForms.rootDefinition: having the ability or quality needed to do something

wordForms.definition: the ability to produce original and imaginative ideas
wordForms.example: The job requires a high level of creativity and initiative.
wordForms.rootDefinition: having the ability to produce new and original ideas

wordForms.definition: the state of having great variety
wordForms.example: The school celebrates cultural diversity with an annual festival.
wordForms.rootDefinition: showing a great deal of variety or difference

wordForms.definition: the state of having the same rights and opportunities
wordForms.example: The organisation campaigns for gender equality in the workplace.
wordForms.rootDefinition: the same in value, rights, or opportunities

wordForms.definition: the ability to adapt or change to suit new conditions
wordForms.example: Employers value flexibility in their staff.
wordForms.rootDefinition: able to change or be changed easily

wordForms.definition: the quality of being willing to give more than expected
wordForms.example: Her generosity towards the local charity was well known.
wordForms.rootDefinition: willing to give or share more than is necessary

wordForms.definition: the state of being fully developed or grown
wordForms.example: She handled the difficult situation with great maturity.
wordForms.rootDefinition: fully grown or developed in body or mind

wordForms.definition: the state or fact of being likely to happen or exist
wordForms.example: There is a real possibility that the match will be cancelled.
wordForms.rootDefinition: able to be done or likely to happen

wordForms.definition: the rate at which useful work or results are produced
wordForms.example: Working from home can increase productivity for many people.
wordForms.rootDefinition: producing a large amount of goods or useful results

wordForms.definition: the state of being accountable for something
wordForms.example: It is everyone's responsibility to protect the environment.
wordForms.rootDefinition: having a duty to deal with something or care for someone

multipleChoiceExercises.sentence: Working from home can greatly improve employee ___.
The company promotes ___ and inclusion in all its hiring decisions.
Taking ___ for your mistakes is a sign of strong character.
The ___ of her response showed how well prepared she was.
There is a real ___ that the project will be completed ahead of schedule.
The ___ of the team made it easy to switch roles when needed.

transformExercises.sentence: The company's ___ in handling the crisis impressed everyone. (MATURE)
The school encourages ___ by giving students open-ended projects. (CREATIVE)
Her ___ knew no bounds — she donated half her salary to charity. (GENEROUS)
The new system has the ___ to handle thousands of users at once. (CAPABLE)
Cultural ___ in the workplace leads to more innovative solutions. (DIVERSE)
